PRIMARY OBJECTIVE\r\n\r\nPerform a full-system audit and implementation pass focused on:\r\n\r\n* visual cohesion;\r\n* user-experience cohesion;\r\n* navigation clarity;\r\n* narrative and terminology consistency;\r\n* reusable design-system components;\r\n* animation consistency;\r\n* responsive behavior;\r\n* content generalization;\r\n* accessibility;\r\n* loading, empty, error, and success states;\r\n* interface hierarchy;\r\n* interaction feedback;\r\n* technical cleanup;\r\n* removal of temporary or placeholder-feeling implementation;\r\n* consistency between public-facing screens and in-session experiences;\r\n* integration of all existing functionality into one unified product experience.\r\n\r\nThe application should feel as though it was designed and implemented from a single comprehensive product specification, even if the underlying features were originally built at different times.\r\n\r\nDo not remove existing working features merely to simplify the polishing effort.\r\n\r\nDo not replace meaningful functionality with mockups.\r\n\r\nDo not reduce the depth of the system.\r\n\r\nImprove, unify, and complete what is already present.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# 2. CRITICAL CONTENT REQUIREMENT: KEEP MOST TEXT GENERIC AND REUSABLE\r\n\r\nMost interface text must not be written specifically for one person, one couple, one relationship, one date, one event, or one BEGIN WITH A COMPLETE APPLICATION AUDIT\r\n\r\nBefore making broad changes, inspect the entire repository.\r\n\r\nIdentify:\r\n\r\n* application framework and version;\r\n* routing structure;\r\n* page hierarchy;\r\n* layout components;\r\n* global providers;\r\n* design tokens;\r\n* styling approach;\r\n* animation libraries;\r\n* component libraries;\r\n* state-management approach;\r\n* server and client boundaries;\r\n* API routes;\r\n* database or persistence layer;\r\n* authentication behavior;\r\n* content schemas;\r\n* Tall Tale schemas;\r\n* session schemas;\r\n* invitation flows;\r\n* existing responsive breakpoints;\r\n* accessibility infrastructure;\r\n* error boundaries;\r\n* loading boundaries;\r\n* reusable components;\r\n* duplicated implementations;\r\n* orphaned pages;\r\n* incomplete routes;\r\n* placeholder data;\r\n* hard-coded personal content;\r\n* mock content incorrectly presented as production content;\r\n* inconsistent naming;\r\n* visual regressions;\r\n* dead code;\r\n* console errors;\r\n* build warnings;\r\n* lint errors;\r\n* test failures.\r\n\r\nSearch the repository for:\r\n\r\n* personal names;\r\n* event-specific phrases;\r\n* placeholder text;\r\n* TODO markers;\r\n* FIXME markers;\r\n* temporary styling;\r\n* duplicated colors;\r\n* duplicated spacing values;\r\n* arbitrary z-index values;\r\n* inconsistent border radii;\r\n* inconsistent shadows;\r\n* direct animation implementations;\r\n* hard-coded dimensions;\r\n* hard-coded session data;\r\n* hard-coded story data;\r\n* unfinished handlers;\r\n* disabled buttons;\r\n* routes that are not reachable through navigation.\r\n\r\nCreate or update an internal implementation checklist in the repository if an appropriate development documentation location already exists.\r\n\r\nDo not stop after the audit. page.\r\n\r\n## 5.3 Consistent component states\r\n\r\nEvery interactive component should have intentional states for:\r\n\r\n* default;\r\n* hover;\r\n* active or pressed;\r\n* focus-visible;\r\n* ESTABLISH A COHERENT VISUAL IDENTITY\r\n\r\nThe product can remain immersive and story-driven without sacrificing usability.\r\n\r\nThe visual identity should balance:\r\n\r\n* narrative atmosphere;\r\n* modern application clarity;\r\n* readable typography;\r\n* strong hierarchy;\r\n* restrained decorative treatment;\r\n* responsive layouts;\r\n* accessible contrast;\r\n* consistent component behavior.\r\n\r\nUse thematic visual elements intentionally.\r\n\r\nPotential thematic elements may include:\r\n\r\n* parchment;\r\n* books;\r\n* maps;\r\n* ink;\r\n* compass motifs;\r\n* subtle nautical details;\r\n* seals;\r\n* chapters;\r\n* story markers;\r\n* softly animated atmospheric backgrounds;\r\n* restrained glass, paper, or layered-surface effects.\r\n\r\nDo not place every known maritime object onto every screen. The application should evoke an adventure, not resemble a pirate-themed restaurant menu designed during a caffeine emergency.\r\n\r\nDecorative elements must not:\r\n\r\n* block controls;\r\n* reduce text contrast;\r\n* make mobile layouts crowded;\r\n* slow down core interactions;\r\n* create confusing hit targets;\r\n* interfere with keyboard navigation;\r\n* obscure loading or error states.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# 7. UNIFY GLOBAL APPLICATION STRUCTURE\r\n\r\nAudit the overall shell of the application.\r\n\r\nCreate or improve a shared application shell containing the appropriate combination of:\r\n\r\n* top navigation;\r\n* side navigation;\r\n* mobile navigation;\r\n* page container;\r\n* global background treatment;\r\n* route transition area;\r\n* account or profile access;\r\n* notifications;\r\n* help access;\r\n* session status;\r\n* consistent footer where appropriate.\r\n\r\n## 7.1 Navigation requirements\r\n\r\nNavigation must clearly answer:\r\n\r\n* Where am I?\r\n* What can I do here?\r\n* How do I go back?\r\n* How do I return home?\r\n* How do I access my active session?\r\n* How do I resume an unfinished Tall Tale?\r\n* How do I access saved content?\r\n* How do I reach settings?\r\n* How do I leave or end a session?\r\n* What actions are available only to a host?\r\n\r\nCurrent route or section state must be visually clear.\r\n\r\nAvoid having multiple unrelated navigation systems competing on the same screen.\r\n\r\n## 7.2 Route continuity\r\n\r\nVerify every major page is reachable through the interface.\r\n\r\nVerify back navigation behaves sensibly.\r\n\r\nVerify browser refresh does not destroy legitimate state.\r\n\r\nVerify deep links work where supported.\r\n\r\nVerify authenticated and unauthenticated routes behave consistently.\r\n\r\nVerify users cannot accidentally enter a broken intermediate setup state.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# 8. POLISH THE LANDING AND ONBOARDING EXPERIENCE\r\n\r\nThe landing experience should clearly communicate:\r\n\r\n* what the product is;\r\n* what a Tall Tale is;\r\n* what users can create or experience;\r\n* how hosting differs from joining;\r\n* what types of experiences are supported;\r\n* how quickly a user can begin.\r\n\r\nAvoid default messaging that makes the entire platform sound built for one specific couple or event.\r\n\r\nUse broad, inviting examples.\r\n\r\nPossible presentation structure:\r\n\r\n1. clear product statement;\r\n2. primary action;\r\n3. secondary join action;\r\n4. concise explanation of Tall Tales;\r\n5. example experience categories;\r\n6. how the experience works;\r\n7. saved or reusable experiences;\r\n8. trust, privacy, or control information where applicable.\r\n\r\n## 8.1 Calls to action\r\n\r\nPrimary actions should be unambiguous, such as:\r\n\r\n* Create an Experience\r\n* Explore Tall Tales\r\n* Join a Session\r\n* Continue Current Session\r\n\r\nDo not use vague buttons such as â€œGo,â€ â€œNext,â€ or â€œStartâ€ when the action context is not obvious.\r\n\r\n## 8.2 Onboarding\r\n\r\nIf onboarding exists, ensure it:\r\n\r\n* is short;\r\n* is skippable where appropriate;\r\n* can be revisited;\r\n* does not assume romantic intent;\r\n* explains host and participant roles;\r\n* explains Tall Tales;\r\n* explains saving and resuming;\r\n* explains invitations without overwhelming the user.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# 9. UNIFY TALL TALE DISCOVERY, PREVIEW, AND SELECTION\r\n\r\nTall Tales should have a consistent information model across every page.\r\n\r\nEach Tall Tale may include:\r\n\r\n* title;\r\n* short description;\r\n* theme;\r\n* experience category;\r\n* estimated duration;\r\n* participant range;\r\n* tone;\r\n* difficulty or complexity where relevant;\r\n* required materials;\r\n* preparation requirements;\r\n* location requirements;\r\n* accessibility considerations;\r\n* number of chapters;\r\n* replayability;\r\n* customization availability;\r\n* content warnings where relevant;\r\n* saved or favorite status.\r\n\r\nDo not show different, contradictory metadata depending on which screen the user happens to visit.\r\n\r\n## 9.1 Tall Tale cards\r\n\r\nTall Tale cards should use one coherent component family.\r\n\r\nCards should have:\r\n\r\n* consistent aspect ratios;\r\n* consistent title placement;\r\n* readable summaries;\r\n* clear metadata;\r\n* clear hover and focus behavior;\r\n* obvious selection state;\r\n* obvious primary action;\r\n* responsive behavior;\r\n* meaningful empty-image handling.\r\n\r\n## 9.2 Preview experience\r\n\r\nA Tall Tale preview should clearly distinguish between:\r\n\r\n* system metadata;\r\n* authored story preview;\r\n* user customization;\r\n* session-specific settings.\r\n\r\nUsers should understand what they are choosing before beginning.\r\n\r\n## 9.3 Filtering and search\r\n\r\nWhere applicable, allow users to search or filter by:\r\n\r\n* theme;\r\n* duration;\r\n* group size;\r\n* experience type;\r\n* setting;\r\n* tone;\r\n* preparation level;\r\n* saved or favorite status.\r\n\r\nSearch and filters should remain usable on mobile.\r\n\r\nFilter states should be clear and removable.\r\n\r\nA â€œno resultsâ€ state should explain how to broaden the search.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# 10. MAKE SESSION CREATION FEEL LIKE ONE GUIDED FLOW\r\n\r\nAudit the complete create-session or create-experience flow.\r\n\r\nIt should feel like one intentional process, not a series of unrelated forms.\r\n\r\nPotential stages may include:\r\n\r\n1. choose or create a Tall Tale;\r\n2. select experience type;\r\n3. choose participants or group size;\r\n4. configure story options;\r\n5. enter optional personalization;\r\n6. set privacy or invitation behavior;\r\n7. review setup;\r\n8. create session;\r\n9. invite participants;\r\n10. begin when ready.\r\n\r\nUse existing functionality and documented flow. Do not impose this exact step count if the architecture defines another process.\r\n\r\n## 10.1 Progress indication\r\n\r\nMulti-step flows should show:\r\n\r\n* current step;\r\n* completed steps;\r\n* remaining steps;\r\n* whether the user can return to prior steps;\r\n* whether changes are saved.\r\n\r\n## 10.2 Validation\r\n\r\nValidation should be:\r\n\r\n* immediate where helpful;\r\n* specific;\r\n* human-readable;\r\n* located near the relevant field;\r\n* preserved if the user navigates backward;\r\n* accessible to screen readers.\r\n\r\nAvoid generic errors such as â€œSomething went wrongâ€ when a specific explanation is available.\r\n\r\n## 10.3 Review screen\r\n\r\nBefore committing a session, show a clear summary of:\r\n\r\n* selected Tall Tale;\r\n* participant configuration;\r\n* duration;\r\n* customization;\r\n* host controls;\r\n* invitation behavior;\r\n* any required preparation;\r\n* any irreversible choices.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# 11. MAKE THE IN-SESSION TALL TALE INTERFACE THE CENTRAL EXPERIENCE\r\n\r\nThe in-session Tall Tale interface is a major product surface and should feel like the culmination of the entire platform.\r\n\r\nThe previously established immersive book-centered Tall Tale interface should remain the primary player-facing presentation during a session, rather than reverting to a generic sequence of disconnected cards.\r\n\r\nThe in-session experience should feel like an interactive storybook, adventure journal, or guided narrative interface.\r\n\r\n## 11.1 Core layout\r\n\r\nThe chapters;\r\n* passages;\r\n* decisions;\r\n* activities;\r\n* prompts;\r\n* reveals;\r\n* transitions;\r\n* completion states.\r\n\r\nProgression should not feel like merely clicking through a stack completion state persists;\r\n* session ownership and permissions are enforced.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# 12. CONNECT THE PUBLIC WEBSITE TO THE IN-SESSION EXPERIENCE\r\n\r\nThe landing pages, dashboards, Tall Tale library, creation flow, invitation flow, and in-sessio…24 tokens truncated…riented labels.\r\n\r\nExamples:\r\n\r\n* Save Changes\r\n* Create Session\r\n* Send Invitation\r\n* Begin Tall Tale\r\n* Resume Story\r\n* Leave Session\r\n* End Session\r\n* Preview Experience\r\n* Add Participant\r\n* Remove Filter\r\n* Return to Library\r\n\r\n## 14.3 Destructive actions\r\n\r\nDestructive actions must clearly explain consequences.\r\n\r\nUse confirmation dialogs for actions such as:\r\n\r\n* ending a session;\r\n* deleting a Tall Tale;\r\n* deleting a saved experience;\r\n* removing participants;\r\n* resetting progress;\r\n* discarding unsaved customization.\r\n\r\nDo not use vague confirmation text such as â€œAre you sure?â€ without naming the action.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# 15. IMPLEMENT COMPLETE UX STATES\r\n\r\nEvery major screen and data-driven component must support appropriate states.\r\n\r\n## 15.1 Loading states\r\n\r\nUse:\r\n\r\n* skeletons for content-heavy pages;\r\n* inline spinners for local actions;\r\n* disabled action controls during submission;\r\n* progress messaging for genuinely long operations.\r\n\r\nAvoid full-page spinners for small local updates.\r\n\r\nAvoid layout shifts when content appears.\r\n\r\n## 15.2 Empty states\r\n\r\nCreate meaningful empty states for:\r\n\r\n* no saved Tall Tales;\r\n* no active sessions;\r\n* no completed stories;\r\n* no invitations;\r\n* no participants;\r\n* no search results;\r\n* no favorites;\r\n* no custom experiences;\r\n* no available history.\r\n\r\nEach empty state should explain what the area is and provide an appropriate next action.\r\n\r\n## 15.3 Error states\r\n\r\nErrors should:\r\n\r\n* state what failed;\r\n* preserve user-entered data;\r\n* provide recovery actions;\r\n* distinguish validation errors from network or server errors;\r\n* avoid exposing raw stack traces;\r\n* log technical details appropriately;\r\n* remain accessible.\r\n\r\n## 15.4 Success states\r\n\r\nUse clear success feedback for:\r\n\r\n* saved changes;\r\n* session creation;\r\n* invitation copied;\r\n* invitation sent;\r\n* Tall Tale saved;\r\n* story completed;\r\n* customization published;\r\n* settings updated.\r\n\r\nAvoid excessive toast spam.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# 16. ANIMATION AND MOTION SYSTEM\r\n\r\nAudit all existing animation behavior and libraries.\r\n\r\nConsolidate motion into a coherent system.\r\n\r\nDefine standard motion categories:\r\n\r\n* microinteraction;\r\n* panel transition;\r\n* route transition;\r\n* modal transition;\r\n* chapter transition;\r\n* page-turn effect;\r\n* success celebration;\r\n* ambient background motion.\r\n\r\nDefine consistent:\r\n\r\n* durations;\r\n* easing;\r\n* stagger behavior;\r\n* entrance direction;\r\n* exit behavior;\r\n* reduced-motion alternatives.\r\n\r\n## 16.1 Motion principles\r\n\r\nAnimations should:\r\n\r\n* clarify state changes;\r\n* reinforce hierarchy;\r\n* make transitions feel continuous;\r\n* support the story atmosphere;\r\n* remain performant;\r\n* avoid blocking interaction;\r\n* respect `prefers-reduced-motion`.\r\n\r\nAnimations should not:\r\n\r\n* replay constantly without purpose;\r\n* move every element independently;\r\n* cause text to become difficult to read;\r\n* delay routine navigation;\r\n* create nausea-inducing parallax;\r\n* make the interface feel like a theme-park kiosk having a nervous breakdown.\r\n\r\n## 16.2 Performance\r\n\r\nPrefer GPU-friendly properties such as opacity and transform.\r\n\r\nAvoid unnecessary layout-triggering animation.\r\n\r\nTest on lower-power devices and mobile emulation.\r\n\r\nDo not ship giant animation assets without optimization.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# 17. RESPONSIVE DESIGN\r\n\r\nAudit every major route at multiple widths.\r\n\r\nAt minimum, test representative sizes for:\r\n\r\n* small mobile;\r\n* large mobile;\r\n* tablet portrait;\r\n* tablet landscape;\r\n* standard laptop;\r\n* large desktop;\r\n* ultrawide display.\r\n\r\n## 17.1 Mobile expectations\r\n\r\nMobile layouts must not feel like compressed desktop layouts.\r\n\r\nEnsure:\r\n\r\n* controls have adequate touch targets;\r\n* bottom or drawer navigation is considered where appropriate;\r\n* modals fit vertically;\r\n* forms do not overflow;\r\n* sticky actions do not cover content;\r\n* the book interface remains usable;\r\n* page-turn interactions have touch-friendly alternatives;\r\n* tooltips are not required for essential information;\r\n* decorative backgrounds do not reduce performance;\r\n* text remains readable without zooming.\r\n\r\n## 17.2 Book interface responsiveness\r\n\r\nThe Tall Tale book interface may adapt differently across breakpoints.\r\n\r\nDesktop may use:\r\n\r\n* two-page spread;\r\n* supporting side panels;\r\n* expanded chapter navigation.\r\n\r\nMobile may use:\r\n\r\n* single-page reading;\r\n* horizontal or controlled page navigation;\r\n* collapsible session controls;\r\n* compact progress display;\r\n* bottom-sheet actions.\r\n\r\nDo not force a tiny two-page book onto a phone merely because it looked impressive at 1440 pixels.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# 18. ACCESSIBILITY\r\n\r\nPerform an accessibility pass across the system.\r\n\r\nVerify:\r\n\r\n* semantic HTML;\r\n* logical heading hierarchy;\r\n* keyboard navigation;\r\n* visible focus indicators;\r\n* correct button and link usage;\r\n* form labels;\r\n* error associations;\r\n* accessible names for icon controls;\r\n* modal focus trapping;\r\n* focus restoration;\r\n* menu keyboard behavior;\r\n* sufficient color contrast;\r\n* screen-reader-friendly status announcements;\r\n* reduced-motion support;\r\n* alt text strategy;\r\n* non-color status cues;\r\n* accessible drag-and-drop alternatives where applicable.\r\n\r\nDo not use clickable `<div>` elements where buttons or links are appropriate.\r\n\r\nDecorative images should not create screen-reader clutter.\r\n\r\nInteractive story elements must remain usable without a mouse.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# 19. PERFORMANCE AND TECHNICAL QUALITY\r\n\r\nPolish includes technical behavior, not merely visual decoration.\r\n\r\nAudit:\r\n\r\n* unnecessary re-renders;\r\n* oversized client bundles;\r\n* duplicate dependencies;\r\n* unused imports;\r\n* unoptimized images;\r\n* excessive animation cost;\r\n* repeated data fetching;\r\n* waterfalls;\r\n* missing cache behavior;\r\n* unstable keys;\r\n* hydration mismatches;\r\n* console warnings;\r\n* memory leaks;\r\n* event listener cleanup;\r\n* stale state;\r\n* race conditions;\r\n* duplicate submissions;\r\n* client and server boundary violations.\r\n\r\nUse lazy loading where appropriate, but do not create fragmented loading behavior.\r\n\r\nOptimize Tall Tale media and cover assets.\r\n\r\nPreserve SEO and metadata behavior for public routes where relevant.\r\n\r\nAdd or improve route-level metadata, page titles, and descriptions.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# 20. DATA AND CONTENT INTEGRITY\r\n\r\nEnsure polished interfaces are connected to real application data.\r\n\r\nDo not create attractive components that display separate hard-coded copies of data already available through the system.\r\n\r\nEstablish clear sources for:\r\n\r\n* Tall Tale metadata;\r\n* session metadata;\r\n* participant data;\r\n* progress;\r\n* invitations;\r\n* completion status;\r\n* customization;\r\n* saved experiences;\r\n* user settings.\r\n\r\nWhere demo data is necessary:\r\n\r\n* identify it clearly;\r\n* isolate it from production behavior;\r\n* avoid embedding personal details;\r\n* use varied, generic examples;\r\n* ensure it does not overwrite real data.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# 21. USER SETTINGS AND PREFERENCES\r\n\r\nWhere supported, unify settings for:\r\n\r\n* appearance;\r\n* motion;\r\n* sound;\r\n* text effects;\r\n* notifications;\r\n* privacy;\r\n* session defaults;\r\n* accessibility;\r\n* content preferences.\r\n\r\nChanges should persist correctly.\r\n\r\nSettings should affect all relevant screens, not merely the page on which they were changed.\r\n\r\nExamples:\r\n\r\n* reduced motion should affect route transitions, page turns, and live writing;\r\n* sound settings should affect all story audio;\r\n* theme settings should affect public and in-session interfaces;\r\n* text-speed settings should affect all animated story text.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# 22. SOUND AND AUDIO BEHAVIOR\r\n\r\nIf the application includes sound:\r\n\r\n* provide clear global controls;\r\n* never autoplay disruptive audio without appropriate consent;\r\n* remember user preferences;\r\n* use sound as optional enhancement;\r\n* provide captions or text equivalents where meaningful;\r\n* prevent overlapping audio;\r\n* stop audio when leaving the relevant context;\r\n* ensure route changes do not leave orphaned playback.\r\n\r\nAmbient audio, page sounds, and celebration sounds should be subtle and consistent.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# 23. INVITATION, LOBBY, AND JOINING EXPERIENCE\r\n\r\nPolish the entire joining flow.\r\n\r\nA participant should understand:\r\n\r\n* what they are joining;\r\n* who is hosting;\r\n* whether sign-in is required;\r\n* what name or identifier they will use;\r\n* whether the session has started;\r\n* whether the session is full;\r\n* whether the invitation is expired;\r\n* whether approval is required;\r\n* what happens after joining.\r\n\r\n## 23.1 Invitation presentation\r\n\r\nInvitations should use generic system language with dynamic session data.\r\n\r\nDo not assume every invitation is for a date, anniversary, or romantic partner.\r\n\r\n## 23.2 Lobby\r\n\r\nThe lobby should clearly show:\r\n\r\n* session title;\r\n* Tall Tale;\r\n* host;\r\n* participants;\r\n* readiness;\r\n* session settings summary;\r\n* preparation requirements;\r\n* start controls;\r\n* leave controls;\r\n* connection state.\r\n\r\nWaiting states should feel intentional, not broken.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# 24. DASHBOARDS, HISTORY, AND CONTINUATION\r\n\r\nAny dashboard or home screen should prioritize useful next actions.\r\n\r\nPossible priority order:\r\n\r\n1. continue active session;\r\n2. respond to invitations;\r\n3. resume unfinished setup;\r\n4. create a new experience;\r\n5. browse Tall Tales;\r\n6. view recent or completed experiences;\r\n7. manage saved content.\r\n\r\nAvoid filling the top of the dashboard with decorative statistics that do not help users decide what to do next.\r\n\r\nCompleted and active experiences must be clearly distinguished.\r\n\r\nResume buttons must lead to the correct state.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# 25. COMPLETION AND RECAP EXPERIENCE\r\n\r\nFinishing a Tall Tale should feel meaningful.\r\n\r\nThe completion experience may include, where supported:\r\n\r\n* completion message;\r\n* session recap;\r\n* decisions made;\r\n* chapters completed;\r\n* participant contributions;\r\n* saved memories;\r\n* photos or notes;\r\n* replay options;\r\n* related Tall Tales;\r\n* return-to-dashboard action;\r\n* shareable summary;\r\n* private history entry.\r\n\r\nUse broadly applicable default language.\r\n\r\nA romantic Tall Tale may have romantic authored completion content, but the system-level completion screen must support any experience category.\r\n\r\nDo not trap the user in a celebration animation with no obvious exit.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# 26. CONSISTENT ICONOGRAPHY AND MEDIA\r\n\r\nAudit icons and visual assets.\r\n\r\nUse a consistent icon library or clearly coordinated icon set.\r\n\r\nAvoid mixing:\r\n\r\n* filled icons;\r\n* outline icons;\r\n* emoji;\r\n* unrelated illustration styles;\r\n* random stock images;\r\n* different corner treatments.\r\n\r\nUse emoji only where intentionally part of the product voice, not as a substitute for interface design.\r\n\r\nEnsure images:\r\n\r\n* have appropriate dimensions;\r\n* are optimized;\r\n* do not stretch;\r\n* have stable aspect ratios;\r\n* include suitable fallbacks;\r\n* use meaningful alt text where necessary.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# 27. CLEAN UP MODALS, DRAWERS, AND OVERLAYS\r\n\r\nAll overlays should behave consistently.\r\n\r\nVerify:\r\n\r\n* backdrop behavior;\r\n* escape-key handling;\r\n* outside-click behavior;\r\n* focus trapping;\r\n* focus restoration;\r\n* mobile sizing;\r\n* scroll locking;\r\n* layering;\r\n* close-button placement;\r\n* title hierarchy;\r\n* destructive action placement.\r\n\r\nDo not allow multiple modal systems to behave differently.\r\n\r\nAvoid stacking modals unless absolutely necessary.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# 28. REMOVE DISCONNECTED OR REDUNDANT INTERFACES\r\n\r\nIdentify screens that duplicate the same purpose.\r\n\r\nExamples may include:\r\n\r\n* multiple Tall Tale browsing pages;\r\n* old and new in-session interfaces;\r\n* duplicate profile settings;\r\n* duplicate create-session flows;\r\n* orphaned prototypes;\r\n* old card-based story screens that conflict with the central book interface.\r\n\r\nDo not delete them blindly.\r\n\r\nDetermine whether they are:\r\n\r\n* obsolete;\r\n* still routed;\r\n* used by tests;\r\n* used by specific roles;\r\n* part of an unfinished migration.\r\n\r\nConsolidate behavior safely.\r\n\r\nThe book-centered interface should be the canonical in-session Tall Tale player experience unless a governing specification explicitly requires another presentation for a special mode.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# 29. ERROR PREVENTION AND CONFIRMATION DESIGN\r\n\r\nPrevent common mistakes.\r\n\r\nExamples:\r\n\r\n* warn before leaving with unsaved changes;\r\n* prevent hosts from unintentionally ending an active session;\r\n* prevent duplicate invitations;\r\n* prevent duplicate progression;\r\n* prevent submitting incomplete required setup;\r\n* prevent starting when required participants or content are missing;\r\n* explain why unavailable actions are disabled;\r\n* preserve form state after recoverable errors.\r\n\r\nAvoid unnecessary confirmations for harmless actions.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# 30. TESTING REQUIREMENTS\r\n\r\nAdd or update tests appropriate to the existing stack.\r\n\r\nAt minimum, cover critical paths such as:\r\n\r\n* landing to Tall Tale discovery;\r\n* Tall Tale preview;\r\n* create-session flow;\r\n* invitation or join flow;\r\n* lobby behavior;\r\n* beginning a Tall Tale;\r\n* chapter progression;\r\n* refresh and resume;\r\n* completion;\r\n* permissions for host-only actions;\r\n* settings persistence;\r\n* reduced-motion behavior where testable;\r\n* responsive navigation behavior;\r\n* empty and error states.\r\n\r\nRun:\r\n\r\n* formatting;\r\n* linting;\r\n* type checking;\r\n* unit tests;\r\n* integration tests;\r\n* end-to-end tests where configured;\r\n* production build.\r\n\r\nResolve errors introduced by this work.\r\n\r\nDo not claim completion while the production build fails.\r\n\r\nDocument any pre-existing failures that cannot be safely corrected within this scope.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# 31. ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA\r\n\r\nThe work is complete only when all of the following are true.\r\n\r\n## 31.1 Product cohesion\r\n\r\n* The application feels like one product.\r\n* Public, dashboard, setup, lobby, and in-session screens share a coherent design language.\r\n* Terminology is consistent.\r\n* Navigation is understandable.\r\n* The central Tall Tale interface feels connected to the rest of the application.\r\n\r\n## 31.2 Content generalization\r\n\r\n* Most system copy is not specific to one person or one event.\r\n* Hard-coded personal names and relationship-specific assumptions are removed from general UI.\r\n* Event-specific content appears only where contextually appropriate.\r\n* Demo content is generic and clearly separated from system behavior.\r\n* Romantic language is not used as the universal default.\r\n\r\n## 31.3 Visual quality\r\n\r\n* Typography is consistent.\r\n* Spacing is consistent.\r\n* Components use shared variants.\r\n* States are complete.\r\n* Decorative elements support rather than obstruct usability.\r\n* Mobile layouts are intentionally designed.\r\n* The book-centered story experience remains visually important.\r\n\r\n## 31.4 Interaction quality\r\n\r\n* Buttons clearly communicate actions.\r\n* Loading, empty, error, and success states are implemented.\r\n* Forms validate clearly.\r\n* Transitions are consistent.\r\n* Reduced-motion preferences are supported.\r\n* Host and participant actions are distinguishable.\r\n\r\n## 31.5 Technical quality\r\n\r\n* No new console errors.\r\n* No new type errors.\r\n* No broken routes.\r\n* No major regressions.\r\n* Production build succeeds.\r\n* Critical tests pass.\r\n* Data is not replaced with visual-only mocks.\r\n* Existing documented functionality is preserved.\r\n\r\n## 31.6 Accessibility\r\n\r\n* Keyboard navigation works.\r\n* Focus states are visible.\r\n* Forms have labels.\r\n* Dialogs manage focus.\r\n* Contrast is acceptable.\r\n* Icon-only actions have accessible labels.\r\n* Essential content does not rely solely on animation, sound, hover, or color.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# 32. IMPLEMENTATION APPROACH\r\n\r\nUse an organized implementation sequence.\r\n\r\nRecommended sequence:\r\n\r\n1. inspect repository and documentation;\r\n2. map routes, features, and shared systems;\r\n3. identify inconsistencies and obsolete interfaces;\r\n4. consolidate design tokens;\r\n5. consolidate shared components;\r\n6. improve global application shell and navigation;\r\n7. standardize terminology and system copy;\r\n8. generalize hard-coded event-specific language;\r\n9. polish landing and onboarding;\r\n10. polish Tall Tale discovery and previews;\r\n11. polish session creation;\r\n12. polish invitation and lobby flows;\r\n13. polish the book-centered in-session interface;\r\n14. unify animations;\r\n15. implement complete UX states;\r\n16. complete responsive pass;\r\n17. complete accessibility pass;\r\n18. complete performance and technical cleanup;\r\n19. add or update tests;\r\n20. run full verification;\r\n21. document work performed.\r\n\r\nYou may adjust the exact order where repository dependencies require it.\r\n\r\nDo not attempt to hide incomplete integration behind styling.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# 33. REQUIRED FINAL DELIVERABLES\r\n\r\nAt the end of the work, provide a detailed implementation report containing:\r\n\r\n## A. Audit summary\r\n\r\n* major systems inspected;\r\n* major inconsistencies found;\r\n* obsolete or duplicate interfaces found;\r\n* hard-coded personalization found;\r\n* important architectural observations.\r\n\r\n## B. Changes made\r\n\r\nGroup changes by:\r\n\r\n* design system;\r\n* navigation;\r\n* public pages;\r\n* onboarding;\r\n* Tall Tale library;\r\n* session creation;\r\n* invitations and lobby;\r\n* in-session experience;\r\n* content and terminology;\r\n* animations;\r\n* responsive design;\r\n* accessibility;\r\n* performance;\r\n* tests;\r\n* documentation.\r\n\r\n## C. Files changed\r\n\r\nList major files and briefly explain their purpose.\r\n\r\nDo not dump every generated file if the list becomes meaningless. Focus on significant changes.\r\n\r\n## D. Verification\r\n\r\nReport the exact result of:\r\n\r\n* formatter;\r\n* linter;\r\n* type checker;\r\n* tests;\r\n* production build.\r\n\r\nInclude commands run and whether they passed.\r\n\r\n## E. Remaining issues\r\n\r\nClearly document:\r\n\r\n* known limitations;\r\n* pre-existing failures;\r\n* deferred improvements;\r\n* areas requiring product decisions;\r\n* functionality that could not be safely modified.\r\n\r\nDo not conceal unfinished work.\r\n\r\n## F. Screens or routes reviewed\r\n\r\nList all major routes manually reviewed and the viewport sizes or responsive modes tested.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# 34. IMPORTANT CONSTRAINTS\r\n\r\n* Preserve existing working functionality.\r\n* Preserve real data integrations.\r\n* Follow the repositoryâ€™s established architecture unless there is a strong documented reason to improve it.\r\n* Do not rewrite the entire project unnecessarily.\r\n* Do not introduce a second competing design system.\r\n* Do not scatter additional hard-coded colors and dimensions throughout the code.\r\n* Do not tailor general UI text to one person or one event.\r\n* Do not make the whole product exclusively romantic.\r\n* Do not replace the central Tall Tale book interface with generic story cards.\r\n* Do not make animations mandatory for understanding content.\r\n* Do not remove accessibility behavior.\r\n* Do not leave unfinished placeholder pages.\r\n* Do not use mock data to disguise broken real functionality.\r\n* Do not mark the task complete without running verification.\r\n* Do not create unrelated features outside the polishing and cohesion scope.\r\n* Do not commit secrets, credentials, tokens, local environment files, generated caches, or machine-specific paths.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# 35. FINAL PRODUCT STANDARD\r\n\r\nThe completed application should feel like a polished platform for creating, hosting, joining, and experiencing interactive Tall Tales across many different types of events and relationships.\r\n\r\nA new user should be able to enter the application and understand:\r\n\r\n* what the platform does;\r\n* what a Tall Tale is;\r\n* how to browse one;\r\n* how to create a session;\r\n* how to invite others;\r\n* how to join;\r\n* how to begin;\r\n* how progress works;\r\n* how to resume;\r\n* how to complete an experience.\r\n\r\nThe application should be thematic without becoming confusing, immersive without becoming slow, flexible without becoming vague, and polished without sacrificing the depth already built into the system.\r\n\r\nThe user should feel that every screen belongs to the same story.\r\n\r\nProceed with the complete audit, implementation, testing, and documentation now.\r\n\r\n", "type": "input_text"}]

UNIFY TALL TALE DISCOVERY, PREVIEW, AND SELECTION\r\n0500: \r\n0501: Tall Tales should have a consistent information model across every page.\r\n0502: \r\n0503: Each Tall Tale may include:\r\n0504: \r\n0505: * title;\r\n0506: * short description;\r\n0507: * theme;\r\n0508: * experience category;\r\n0509: * estimated duration;\r\n0510: * participant range;\r\n0511: * tone;\r\n0512: * difficulty or complexity where relevant;\r\n0513: * required materials;\r\n0514: * preparation requirements;\r\n0515: * location requirements;\r\n0516: * accessibility considerations;\r\n0517: * number of chapters;\r\n0518: * replayability;\r\n0519: * customization availability;\r\n0520: * content warnings where relevant;\r\n0521: * saved or favorite status.\r\n0522: \r\n0523: Do not show different, contradictory metadata depending on which screen the user happens to visit.\r\n0524: \r\n0525: ## 9.1 Tall Tale cards\r\n0526: \r\n0527: Tall Tale cards should use one coherent component family.\r\n0528: \r\n0529: Cards should have:\r\n0530: \r\n0531: * consistent aspect ratios;\r\n0532: * consistent title placement;\r\n0533: * readable summaries;\r\n0534: * clear metadata;\r\n0535: * clear hover and focus behavior;\r\n0536: * obvious selection state;\r\n0537: * obvious primary action;\r\n0538: * responsive behavior;\r\n0539: * meaningful empty-image handling.\r\n0540: \r\n0541: ## 9.2 Preview experience\r\n0542: \r\n0543: A Tall Tale preview should clearly distinguish between:\r\n0544: \r\n0545: * system metadata;\r\n0546: * authored story preview;\r\n0547: * user customization;\r\n0548: * session-specific settings.\r\n0549: \r\n0550: Users should understand what they are choosing before beginning.\r\n0551: \r\n0552: ## 9.3 Filtering and search\r\n0553: \r\n0554: Where applicable, allow users to search or filter by:\r\n0555: \r\n0556: * theme;\r\n0557: * duration;\r\n0558: * group size;\r\n0559: * experience type;\r\n0560: * setting;\r\n0561: * tone;\r\n0562: * preparation level;\r\n0563: * saved or favorite status.\r\n0564: \r\n0565: Search and filters should remain usable on mobile.\r\n0566: \r\n0567: Filter states should be clear and removable.\r\n0568: \r\n0569: A â€œno resultsâ€ state should explain how to broaden the search.\r\n0570: \r\n0571: ---\r\n0572: \r\n0573: # 10. MAKE SESSION CREATION FEEL LIKE ONE GUIDED FLOW\r\n0574: \r\n0575: Audit the complete create-session or create-experience flow.\r\n0576: \r\n0577: It should feel like one intentional process, not a series of unrelated forms.\r\n0578: \r\n0579: Potential stages may include:\r\n0580: \r\n0581: 1. choose or create a Tall Tale;\r\n0582: 2. select experience type;\r\n0583: 3. choose participants or group size;\r\n0584: 4. configure story options;\r\n0585: 5. enter optional personalization;\r\n0586: 6. set privacy or invitation behavior;\r\n0587: 7. review setup;\r\n0588: 8. create session;\r\n0589: 9. invite participants;\r\n0590: 10. begin when ready.\r\n0591: \r\n0592: Use existing functionality and documented flow. Do not impose this exact step count if the architecture defines another process.\r\n0593: \r\n0594: ## 10.1 Progress indication\r\n0595: \r\n0596: Multi-step flows should show:\r\n0597: \r\n0598: * current step;\r\n0599: * completed steps;\r\n0600: * remaining steps;\r\n0601: * whether the user can return to prior steps;\r\n0602: * whether changes are saved.\r\n0603: \r\n0604: ## 10.2 Validation\r\n0605: \r\n0606: Validation should be:\r\n0607: \r\n0608: * immediate where helpful;\r\n0609: * specific;\r\n0610: * human-readable;\r\n0611: * located near the relevant field;\r\n0612: * preserved if the user navigates backward;\r\n0613: * accessible to screen readers.\r\n0614: \r\n0615: Avoid generic errors such as â€œSomething went wrongâ€ when a specific explanation is available.\r\n0616: \r\n0617: ## 10.3 Review screen\r\n0618: \r\n0619: Before committing a session, show a clear summary of:\r\n0620: \r\n0621: * selected Tall Tale;\r\n0622: * participant configuration;\r\n0623: * duration;\r\n0624: * customization;\r\n0625: * host controls;\r\n0626: * invitation behavior;\r\n0627: * any required preparation;\r\n0628: * any irreversible choices.\r\n0629: \r\n0630: ---\r\n0631: \r\n0632: # 11. MAKE THE IN-SESSION TALL TALE INTERFACE THE CENTRAL EXPERIENCE\r\n0633: \r\n0634: The in-session Tall Tale interface is a major product surface and should feel like the culmination of the entire platform.\r\n0635: \r\n0636: The previously established immersive book-centered Tall Tale interface should remain the primary player-facing presentation during a session, rather than reverting to a generic sequence of disconnected cards.\r\n0637: \r\n0638: The in-session experience should feel like an interactive storybook, adventure journal, or guided narrative interface.\r\n0639: \r\n0640: ## 11.1 Core layout\r\n0641: \r\n0642: The central book or story surface should be visually dominant.\r\n0643: \r\n0644: Supporting interface regions may include:\r\n0645: \r\n0646: * chapter navigation;\r\n0647: * progress indicator;\r\n0648: * participant status;\r\n0649: * host controls;\r\n0650: * session menu;\r\n0651: * hints;\r\n0652: * optional objectives;\r\n0653: * inventory or story items where supported;\r\n0654: * notes;\r\n0655: * accessibility controls;\r\n0656: * pause or resume controls.\r\n0657: \r\n0658: Supporting controls must not overwhelm the story.\r\n0659: \r\n0660: ## 11.2 Story progression\r\n0661: \r\n0662: Story content should support clear progression through:\r\n0663: \r\n0664: * introductions;\r\n0665: * chapters;\r\n0666: * passages;\r\n0667: * decisions;\r\n0668: * activities;\r\n0669: * prompts;\r\n0670: * reveals;\r\n0671: * transitions;\r\n0672: * completion states.\r\n0673: \r\n0674: Progression should not feel like merely clicking through a stack of generic dashboard cards.\r\n0675: \r\n0676: ## 11.3 Page turning and live writing\r\n0677: \r\n0678: Where these effects are already supported or can be implemented responsibly, improve:\r\n0679: \r\n0680: * page-turn animations;\r\n0681: * text reveal or live-writing animations;\r\n0682: * ink or illustration transitions;\r\n0683: * chapter transitions;\r\n0684: * book opening and closing;\r\n0685: * story completion effects.\r\n0686: \r\n0687: Animation must remain readable and controllable.\r\n0688: \r\n0689: Do not make users wait through slow theatrical animation every time they need to navigate.\r\n0690: \r\n0691: Support reduced-motion preferences.\r\n0692: \r\n0693: Allow long content to render immediately or bypass typing effects.\r\n0694: \r\n0695: ## 11.4 Host and participant roles\r\n0696: \r\n0697: Clearly distinguish:\r\n0698: \r\n0699: * shared story content;\r\n0700: * host-only controls;\r\n0701: * participant actions;\r\n0702: * waiting states;\r\n0703: * synchronized progression;\r\n0704: * voting or choice states;\r\n0705: * reconnect states;\r\n0706: * session ended states.\r\n0707: \r\n0708: Host controls should not appear as ordinary player actions.\r\n0709: \r\n0710: Participants should understand when they are waiting for the host versus when the application has failed.\r\n0711: \r\n0712: ## 11.5 Session persistence\r\n0713: \r\n0714: Verify that:\r\n0715: \r\n0716: * refreshing the page does not unnecessarily lose progress;\r\n0717: * reconnecting returns users to the correct story state;\r\n0718: * active session state is recoverable;\r\n0719: * duplicate progression actions are prevented;\r\n0720: * completion state persists;\r\n0721: * session ownership and permissions are enforced.\r\n0722: \r\n0723: ---\r\n0724: \r\n0725: # 12. CONNECT THE PUBLIC WEBSITE TO THE IN-SESSION EXPERIENCE\r\n0726: \r\n0727: The landing pages, dashboards, Tall Tale library, creation flow, invitation flow, and in-session interface should visually and conceptually lead into one another.\r\n0728: \r\n0729: Use recurring motifs and consistent language.\r\n0730: \r\n0731: Examples:\r\n0732: \r\n0733: * Tall Tale cover art in discovery should correspond to the storybook styling in-session.\r\n0734: * Chapter counts shown during selection should match chapters shown in-session.\r\n0735: * Session names should remain consistent across dashboard, lobby, invitations, and story screens.\r\n0736: * Participant avatars or identifiers should remain consistent.\r\n0737: * Host status should use the same terminology everywhere.\r\n0738: * Progress displayed on dashboards should match actual story progress.\r\n0739: * Resume actions should route to the correct in-session location.\r\n0740: * Completion states should connect to saved memories, recaps, rewards, or history features where those exist.\r\n0741: \r\n0742: Do not let the in-session interface feel like a separate application embedded inside the website.\r\n0743: \r\n0744: ---\r\n0745: \r\n0746: # 13. STANDARDIZE LANGUAGE AND TERMINOLOGY\r\n0747: \r\n0748: Create a terminology map and apply it consistently.\r\n0749: \r\n0750: Determine the official meaning of terms such as:\r\n0751: \r\n0752: * Tall Tale;\r\n0753: * experience;\r\n0754: * adventure;\r\n0755: * story;\r\n0756: * session;\r\n0757: * game;\r\n0758: * host;\r\n0759: * participant;\r\n0760: * player;\r\n0761: * guest;\r\n0762: * crew;\r\n0763: * chapter;\r\n0764: * step;\r\n0765: * prompt;\r\n0766: * activity;\r\n0767: * objective;\r\n0768: * memory;\r\n0769: * recap;\r\n0770: * invite;\r\n0771: * lobby.\r\n0772: \r\n0773: Do not randomly alternate between several terms for the same object.\r\n0774: \r\n0775: Thematic language may be used as secondary flavor, but primary controls must remain understandable.\r\n0776: \r\n0777: For example:\r\n0778: \r\n0779: * Primary label: â€œInvite Participantsâ€\r\n0780: * Optional thematic helper text: â€œGather your crew before the story begins.â€\r\n0781: \r\n0782: Do not make critical controls depend entirely on nautical slang.\r\n0783: \r\n0784: ---\r\n0785: \r\n0786: # 14. IMPROVE ALL SYSTEM COPY\r\n0787: \r\n0788: Audit all visible text.\r\n0789: \r\n0790: Correct:\r\n0791: \r\n0792: * capitalization inconsistencies;\r\n0793: * awkward phrasing;\r\n0794: * unclear labels;\r\n0795: * overly long helper text;\r\n0796: * event-specific assumptions;\r\n0797: * inconsistent punctuation;\r\n0798: * robotic error messages;\r\n0799: * vague buttons;\r\n0800: * duplicated headings;\r\n0801: * placeholder copy;\r\n0802: * development terminology exposed to users.\r\n0803: \r\n0804: ## 14.1 Voice\r\n0805: \r\n0806: The application voice should generally be:\r\n0807: \r\n0808: * warm;\r\n0809: * inviting;\r\n0810: * imaginative;\r\n0811: * clear;\r\n0812: * inclusive;\r\n0813: * lightly adventurous;\r\n0814: * confident;\r\n0815: * not overly childish;\r\n0816: * not excessively romantic by default;\r\n0817: * not dependent on one fandom or event.\r\n0818: \r\n0819: ## 14.2 Button labels\r\n0820: \r\n0821: Use action-oriented labels.\r\n0822: \r\n0823: Examples:\r\n0824: \r\n0825: * Save Changes\r\n0826: * Create Session\r\n0827: * Send Invitation\r\n0828: * Begin Tall Tale\r\n0829: * Resume Story\r\n0830: * Leave Session\r\n0831: * End Session\r\n0832: * Preview Experience\r\n0833: * Add Participant\r\n0834: * Remove Filter\r\n0835: * Return to Library\r\n0836: \r\n0837: ## 14.3 Destructive actions\r\n0838: \r\n0839: Destructive actions must clearly explain consequences.\r\n0840: \r\n0841: Use confirmation dialogs for actions such as:\r\n0842: \r\n0843: * ending a session;\r\n0844: * deleting a Tall Tale;\r\n0845: * deleting a saved experience;\r\n0846: * removing participants;\r\n0847: * resetting progress;\r\n0848: * discarding unsaved customization.\r\n0849: \r\n0850: Do not use vague confirmation text such as â€œAre you sure?â€ without naming the action.\r\n0851: \r\n0852: ---\r\n0853: \r\n0854: # 15. IMPLEMENT COMPLETE UX STATES\r\n0855: \r\n0856: Every major screen and data-driven component must support appropriate states.\r\n0857: \r\n0858: ## 15.1 Loading states\r\n0859: \r\n0860: Use:\r\n0861: \r\n0862: * skeletons for content-heavy pages;\r\n0863: * inline spinners for local actions;\r\n0864: * disabled action controls during submission;\r\n0865: * progress messaging for genuinely long operations.\r\n0866: \r\n0867: Avoid full-page spinners for small local updates.\r\n0868: \r\n0869: Avoid layout shifts when content appears.\r\n0870: \r\n0871: ## 15.2 Empty states\r\n0872: \r\n0873: Create meaningful empty states for:\r\n0874: \r\n0875: * no saved Tall Tales;\r\n0876: * no active sessions;\r\n0877: * no completed stories;\r\n0878: * no invitations;\r\n0879: * no participants;\r\n0880: * no search results;\r\n0881: * no favorites;\r\n0882: * no custom experiences;\r\n0883: * no available history.\r\n0884: \r\n0885: Each empty state should explain what the area is and provide an appropriate next action.\r\n0886: \r\n0887: ## 15.3 Error states\r\n0888: \r\n0889: Errors should:\r\n0890: \r\n0891: * state what failed;\r\n0892: * preserve user-entered data;\r\n0893: * provide recovery actions;\r\n0894: * distinguish validation errors from network or server errors;\r\n0895: * avoid exposing raw stack traces;\r\n0896: * log technical details appropriately;\r\n0897: * remain accessible.\r\n0898: \r\n0899: ## 15.4 Success states\r\n0900: \r\n", "type": "input_text"}]

`http://127.0.0.1:3000/`\n- Player: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/tale/development-forever-treasure`\n- Game Master: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/quartermaster`\n- Development showcase: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/dev/animations`\n- Player sections: `journal`, `chart`, `treasures`, `quests`, `log`, `finale`\n\nThe landing, access gate, player journal workspace, chart, treasure altar, quest ledger, ship's log, finale chamber, and quartermaster dashboard share the physical moonlit-journal design system. The development showcase proves every authored scene, runtime control, fallback, motion mode, and the serial trailer without calling progression APIs. The showcase route returns 404 in production.\n\n## Animation architecture\n\n- `AnimationDirector` is the single transport for scene registration, playback, pause, resume, seek, speed, skip, reverse, cancellation, cleanup, and observable state.\n- GSAP owns authored timeline transforms, SVG drawing, masks, SplitText, MotionPath, labels, and callbacks.\n- Motion owns React mount/unmount, layout, navigation, dialog, and press interactions.\n- StPageFlip owns physical page turns over a shared React page model; reduced motion uses a semantic static page reader.\n- Rive is dynamically loaded from a local WebGL2 binary with CDN fallback disabled and SVG failure fallbacks.\n- Lottie is dynamically loaded from three original local JSON assets and exposes play, pause, segment, speed, direction, visibility, and destruction controls.\n- Full, gentle, and reduced policies preserve state and semantic reading order. Document and element visibility pause expensive runtimes.\n- Procedural Web Audio cues are optional, user-unlocked, disposable, and never block navigation or state transitions.\n\nSee `docs/animation/` for scene inventory, ownership rules, asset contracts, provenance, showcase operation, testing, and performance notes.\n\n## Data and security boundaries\n\n- Existing Prisma, session, allowlist projection, SSE, event ordering, and audit boundaries remain intact.\n- The showcase uses deterministic fictional props and makes no player or GM mutation requests.\n- Local animation assets are validated for expected paths, JSON shape, Rive magic bytes, and remote URL exclusion.\n- No production secrets, private story content, photographs, analytics, or new third-party network calls were added.\n\n## Development commands\n\n- Start: `npm run dev:full`\n- Stop: `npm run dev:stop`\n- Full validation: `npm run validate`\n- Animation asset validation: `npm run assets:validate`\n- Unit tests: `npm test`\n- Production build: `npm run build`\n\nOn a UNC checkout, the PowerShell scripts mirror into `%LOCALAPPDATA%/ForeverTreasureCompanion` so Node, Prisma, Next.js, and Playwright operate on a local filesystem. Local development defaults remain `development-moonwake` for the player and `kato` / `development-captain-only` for the GM; never reuse them outside the disposable local database.\n\n## Validation state\n\n- Strict TypeScript: passing\n- ESLint: passing with zero findings\n- Formatting: passing\n- Animation asset validator: 3 Lottie JSON files, 1 Rive binary, and local SVG fallbacks passing\n- Unit tests: 50 passing across 16 files\n- In-app browser: responsive landing/access/player/GM/showcase flows inspected; clean showcase load has no console warnings or errors\n- Showcase diagnostics: 60 FPS idle estimate, zero-to-one initial development long task depending on cache, expected 1 Rive / 2 Lottie / 1 PageFlip full-mode steady-state counts, no asset failures\n- Horizontal overflow: none at 390, 1440, or 2560 CSS pixels in the showcase audit\n- Full `npm run validate`: passing on 2026-07-17 with 8 applicable Playwright tests passing, 2 intentional heavy-suite project skips, 8 ordered events/audits verified, optimized production build passing, two production restarts passing, and the development showcase returning 404 in production\n- Validation artifacts: `%LOCALAPPDATA%/ForeverTreasureCompanion/validation/artifacts/validation`\n\n## Remaining production work\n\n- Replace the documented development Rive sample and all fictional copy with commissioned/private production art and story content after the repository becomes private.\n- Profile real production art on target low-end mobile hardware and repeat memory traces after sustained navigation.\n- SSE fan-out remains process-local; add Redis or equivalent before multi-instance deployment.\n- Exercise the parallel MySQL migration against MySQL 8 in CI.\n- The finale remains a safe shell until private requirements and ending content are authored.\n\r\n# Architecture\n\n## Phase 3 cinematic companion projection\n\nThe player reads one server-filtered `PublicSnapshot` containing chapters, released hints/annotations, visible map locations/routes, safe artifact states, visible optional mysteries, event-derived log entries, generic finale state, and per-section unseen counts. One SSE connection transports ordered sanitized events; section navigation is client-side and starts no independent polling.\n\nNew normalized records are `MapRoute` and `ViewedContent`. Existing content models gained release, relationship, placement, and safe-label fields rather than duplicating derived display tables. `AudioPreference` now carries the cross-device preference projection; immediate UI choices may also be cached locally for offline startup.\n\n```mermaid\nflowchart LR\n  P[Player journal + animation director] -->|snapshot + access cookie| N[Next.js server]\n  G[Quartermaster] -->|session + CSRF| N\n  N -->|Prisma transaction| D[(SQLite dev / MySQL prod)]\n  N -->|ordered SSE + replay| P\n  N --> A[Audit and save-state log]\n```\n\nServer Components enforce initial access. Route handlers own authentication, validation, snapshots, and SSE. `src/server/progression.ts` is the transaction boundary; `src/domain/story.ts` owns state rules. The UI receives only a public projection, never database rows. In-memory publish accelerates same-process delivery while database replay by sequence remains authoritative after reconnect/restart.\n\nThe client adds one animation director between ordered domain events and presentation. It starts server work immediately, allows only non-authoritative opening/idle motion while pending, and selects a success or failure timeline from the actual response. One queue owns SSE ceremonies, and the public snapshot remains authoritative after skip, cancellation, reconnect, or visual-runtime failure.\n\nMajor dependencies remain deliberately bounded: Prisma for persistence and transactions, bcryptjs for portable hashes, Zod for payload validation, GSAP for cinematic orchestration, Motion for React interaction/presence, StPageFlip for the journal surface, Rive/Lottie for isolated vector assets, Pino for structured redacted logs, and Vitest/Playwright for validation. See `docs/animation/architecture.md` and `docs/animation/library-ownership.md`.\n\nPhase 3 adds an administrative intent layer. `/api/gm/preview` projects without writes, `/api/gm/staging` persists unreleased intent, and `/api/gm/commands` enforces expected sequence and idempotency. `PlayerPresence` supplies expiring evidence. See [admin command pipeline](admin-command-pipeline.md) and [player presence](player-presence-and-sync.md).\n\n## Tall Tale Studio Phase 1\n\nStudio is additive to the original campaign companion. `TallTale` owns identity and catalog metadata; `TaleDraft` owns optimistic autosave state; chapters, blocks, and connections form the editable graph. Publishing validates that graph and writes a complete, checksummed `PublishedTaleVersion.contentSnapshot`. Published rows and referenced asset variants are immutable from the player runtime's perspective.\n\n`src/tall-tale/progression.ts` is the authoritative session engine. Each non-preview `TaleSession` is pinned to one published version and advances through idempotent, ordered events. The player receives its current projection and SSE updates; Captain actions call the same engine. Verification providers all enter through one versioned submission contract, so the simulator and a future paired helper cannot bypass progression rules. See `docs/tall-tale-studio.md`.\n\n## Unified Tall Tale Platform\n\nThe platform extends the Studio/session model rather than copying it. `PlayerProfile`/`PlayerIdentitySession` add durable Player identity; `PlaythroughMembership` is the per-Player authorization and library record; `Invitation`/`InvitationEvent` own single-recipient credential lifecycle; `RevealState` is the historical disclosure ledger; `PlatformRoleAssignment` supplements staff capability scope; and `PlatformAuditEvent` records correlated resource actions. `TaleSession` remains the one playthrough aggregate and retains the exact immutable version relation.\n\n`src/platform/auth.ts`, `policy.ts`, `state.ts`, `invitations.ts`, `libraries.ts`, and `audit.ts` separate authentication, policy, lifecycle, transactional invitation behavior, workspace-specific projections, and safe auditing. Player, Captain, and Creator route families call those services and never serialize a raw aggregate. Compatibility routes call the same progression engine. See [Tall Tale Platform](tall-tale-platform.md) for lifecycle, migration, and security detail.\n\n## Canonical Player journal\n\nThe Player Library is the place where Tall Tales are browsed. The immersive journal is the place where Tall Tales are played. `/player/playthroughs/[id]/journal` is therefore the canonical active, resumed, paused, and completed Player route; compatibility play and archive routes converge on the same component.\n\n`src/tall-tale/progression.ts` remains authoritative. It projects a recursive Player-safe `journal` view from the pinned published snapshot, reveal ledger, ordered events, and current session pointers. `src/tall-tale/journal-contract.ts` defines the ten presentation modes and secret boundary, while `src/tall-tale/journal-page-model.ts` converts only released content into stable physical pages. `PhysicalJournalBook` owns the shared book shell and `TallTaleJournalSession` owns SSE reconciliation, reading position, presentation state, and Player actions. Reading state is persisted separately from progression through the existing Player preferences record.\n\nThis separation keeps Creator content canonical, the Captain engine authoritative, and historical reading immutable. A future location/vision provider can submit a scoped verification outcome through the existing envelope; the resulting ordered event and canonical state projection drive the same journal without a provider-specific Player runtime.\n\r\n\n---RESULT 2---\nExit code: 0\nWall time: 0.3 seconds\nOutput:\n# Shared development documentation\n\n`Development_Docs` is the canonical repository location for shared development references, requirements, architecture notes, design decisions, implementation plans, testing notes, meeting notes, and other planning material that should remain available across Codex sessions and computers.\n\nFiles in this directory are normal Git-tracked project files. The repository's end-of-task synchronization workflow inspects additions, modifications, renames, moves, and deletions here and, after safety checks, can commit and push them through the same approved branch workflow used for `Codex_Chats`. The files are not copied into the conversation archive or packaged into a second store.\n\n## Working from another computer\n\nBefore relying on or editing these documents:\n\n1. Confirm that this is the intended repository and branch with `git remote -v`, `git branch --show-current`, and `git status --short --branch`.\n2. Preserve and review any local modifications. Do not reset, clean, restore, or force-push them away.\n3. Run `git fetch --prune origin`, then compare the branch with its upstream using `git rev-list --left-right --count \"HEAD...@{upstream}\"`.\n4. If the branch is only behind and the working tree is clean, update it with `git pull --ff-only`. If it is ahead, diverged, or locally modified, reconcile the work deliberately before editing these documents.\n5. If Git reports a conflict, preserve both versions and resolve it visibly. Do not push unresolved conflict markers. For an unmergeable binary document, retain clearly named local and remote variants for review when repository conventions permit.\n\nThis pull-before-edit check matters: a Codex session on a different computer must synchronize the latest repository state before treating local `Development_Docs` content as current.\n\n## Safety and change behavior\n\nDo not store secrets, credentials, private keys, access or API tokens, production database exports, private authentication caches, or sensitive company information here unless both the repository and the specific contents have been explicitly approved for that purpose. This repository may be publicly visible. parallel files `Development_Docs/phase_b_implementation_roadmap_v1_0.pdf` and `Development_Docs/vision_waypoint_external_ar_governing_specification_v1_0.pdf` are not task changes.\n\n## Development commands\n\nUse `npm run dev:full` for idempotent setup, migration, progress-preserving seed, and startup. Use `npm run dev:stop` to stop only the recorded process. `npm run dev` is the Next-only path after setup.\n\n## Key state descriptions\n\n- **Library:** grouped parchment cards with Continue/Resume/Open Completed Journal actions; cards remain selectors.\n- **New entry:** closed leather cover, attached latch, intact seal, title/edition, then the opening ceremony and first spread.\n- **Live session:** physical two-page parchment with current Creator-authored content, ink status, contextual chapter/map/artifact/message tools, and objective/action tray.\n- **Reading behind live:** the viewed page does not jump; an announced new-content state and Return to Current Objective control appear.\n- **Mobile:** one readable parchment sheet, compact header, safe-area bottom tools, and touch-safe actions.\n- **Reconnect:** journal stays readable with a reconnection notice and canonical refresh after SSE recovery.\n- **Completed:** the same book opens directly into historical reading with completion treatment, immutable edition/checksum, and no mutation controls.\n\n**Final product rule:** The library helps the Player choose an adventure. The journal is the adventure.\n\r\n\n---RESULT 3---\nExit code: 0\nWall time: 0.2 seconds\nOutput:\n# Design system\n\n## Cinematic companion sections\n\nThe shared ocean-dark, parchment, brass, moonlight, and muted-crimson tokens remain authoritative. Journal uses ink/page hierarchy; Chart uses fog and cartographic marks; Altar uses wood/brass placement; Ledger uses secret-note rhythm; Log uses an engraved timeline; Finale uses a dormant celestial mechanism. All retain the same focus ring, corner language, shadows, texture ceiling, typography scale, and motion tokens.\n\nTokens live in `src/styles/tokens.css`: abyss navy `#061316`, ocean green `#123b3a`, parchment `#d9c69a`, antique brass `#b89551`, crimson `#782f35`, and moonlit teal `#79d2c3`. Georgia/system serif keeps story text readable; Arial is reserved for small operational labels. Paper grain, edge wear, chart lines, brass frames, restrained glow, and vignette are original CSS/SVG materials. Focus is a 3px warm-gold outline. Layers reserve 20â€“35 for persistent chrome, 60 for journal opening, 70 for ceremony controls, and 90 for GM confirmations.\n\n`globals.css` now imports logical material modules: tokens, landing, player, GM, animation runtime, and development showcase. Physical section components own their nearby scene targets. Each GSAP target uses a dedicated inner element so Motion can own the interactive outer wrapper without property conflicts.\n\nMotion is semantic: full, gentle, and reduced retain the same content and order. Reduced mode removes page curl, travel, parallax, particles, and ambient loops but keeps clear state changes. Typography remains one coherent DOM copy during SplitText animation; the split wrappers are reverted after the scene.\n\r\n# Responsive behavior\n\n## Complete shell\n\nDesktop uses the available monitor as a layered story workspace with persistent one-action navigation and compact objective access. Mobile switches to a single content column, labeled bottom navigation, safe-area padding, separate section views, and a compact return-to-clue control. The map always has a text alternative. Artifact inspection becomes a full-screen sheet on narrow screens. Fullscreen mode removes secondary chrome without removing navigation or the objective.\n\nAbove 1100px, chart, open journal, and relic display form a three-region physical workspace. At 1100px and below, bottom navigation exposes one view at a time. At 650px, the journal becomes one vertically readable parchment sheet with safe-area-aware bottom controls. Short phone landscape hides the spine/left sheet and uses a compact single-page composition. Capability choices use CSS width, height, orientation, hover-independent controls, safe-area insets, and `prefers-reduced-motion`; no user-agent sniffing is used.\n\nThe published Tall Tale journal follows the same physical rules while keeping supporting controls secondary. Desktop preserves the dominant open book and contextual side drawers. Compact widths reduce header and tray density while retaining two-page structure. Phone portrait and short landscape use one readable sheet, full-width bottom drawers for chapters/maps/artifacts/messages, touch-safe actions, and a persistent Return to Current Objective control when the reader is behind live progress. Text scaling changes ink size without transforming the entire interface, and reduced motion replaces ceremonial motion with direct, semantically equivalent state changes.\n\nValidated viewports: 1920Ã—1080, 1440Ã—900, 390Ã—844, 430Ã—932, and 844Ã—390. 2560Ã—1440 was layout-reviewed through the same capped workspace rules; automated retained capture remains next-pass work.\n\r\n# Known limitations\n\n- The repository is public. Final story, riddles, personal material, surprise content, production credentials, and private media remain intentionally blocked.\n- SSE fan-out and interactive rate-limit counters are process-local. Database replay is durable, but multi-instance production needs shared pub/sub and rate limiting such as Redis.\n- Tall Tale media uses one local filesystem root. Multi-instance production needs shared object storage; antivirus scanning, resumable large uploads, and asynchronous processing remain hardening work.\n- The additive MySQL migrations are maintained in parity with SQLite but still require a live MySQL 8 integration run before production rollout.\n- The full shared-database mutation workflow runs once in Chromium. Privacy, access gates, the role gateway, accessibility, reduced motion, and mobile behavior also run as read-only WebKit coverage.\n- Captain scheduling persists timezone-aware future state but has no background launch worker; the Captain must explicitly launch a ready or scheduled voyage.\n- SSE access revocation is rechecked on its heartbeat interval, not through an immediate cross-process revocation broadcast.\n- Final designer-authored Rive artwork, licensed audio, real private content, production monitoring, CI hosting, and deployment remain later release work. Current visual assets use documented local development samples and original SVG/CSS/Lottie fallbacks.\n- The future vision provider remains deliberately inactive. Scoped helper pairing, a versioned verification contract, strict rejection rules, and a simulator exist, but the application does not capture camera frames or claim recognition accuracy.\n- Windows LAN access is opt-in and can still be blocked by the host firewall or network policy; localhost is the safe default.\n\r\n# Testing\n\nRun the complete release gate with:\n\n```powershell\nnpm run validate\n```\n\n## Complete validation gate\n\nThe command creates a clean local runtime and disposable database, then runs Prisma generation/migration/seed, database invariants, Prettier, ESLint, TypeScript, the complete Vitest suite, the Chromium/WebKit browser matrix, post-acceptance database checks, production build, and two production restart proofs.\n\nThe live workflow covers player/GM access, rate limiting, cookie isolation, prepare without disclosure, SSE release, ceremony timing/replay/preferences, persistence, duplicate rejection, award/map/solve/undo, offline pause/resume reconciliation, and heartbeat. Command Center tests add preview nonmutation, stale conflict, idempotent replay, all authenticated workspaces, mobile emergency layout, axe accessibility, and visual captures across desktop/tablet/mobile.\n\nArtifacts are ignored and written below the validation runtime at `artifacts/validation`; Command Center screenshots are in `artifacts/validation/command-center`. `PLAYWRIGHT_BASE_URL` and `FOREVER_VALIDATION_PRODUCTION_PORT` may select isolated ports for parallel sessions.\n\n## Integrated validation workflow\n\nThe command mirrors network-share checkouts into a clean local runtime, rebuilds disposable `validation.db`, and runs:\n\n1. Exact lockfile install, Prisma generation, migration deployment, seed, and baseline database invariants.\n2. Prettier, ESLint, strict TypeScript, Vitest component/unit tests, and local animation-asset validation.\n3. application without becoming three divergent products.\r\nThe complete Phase B result will allow a creator to:\r\n1. open the Studio;\r\n2. create a reusable Vision W aypoint;\r\n3. connect the native Capture Companion;\r\n4. record the correct location from several angles and distances;\r\n5. define where players may stand and what they must face;\r\n6. record similar wrong locations and other hard negatives;\r\n7. review or refine important and ignored visual regions;\r\n8. automatically build a calibrated runtime waypoint package;\r\n9. perform guided positive and negative tests;\r\n10.
